intro:
Saul, son of Kish of the tribe of Benjamin, was the first king of Israel. When the elders demanded a king “like all the nations,” the LORD told Samuel that in this they had rejected him from reigning over them, yet granted the request (1 Samuel 8:5-7). Samuel anointed Saul privately and then presented him to the people — a man “taller than any of the people from his shoulders upward” (1 Samuel 9:2; 1 Samuel 10:1). The Spirit of God came upon him and he was acclaimed at Mizpah, “Long live the king!” (1 Samuel 10:24).

His reign began with promise. Roused by the Spirit, he rallied Israel and delivered the men of Jabesh-gilead from the Ammonites (1 Samuel 11:11), and he fought the Philistines throughout his days. But his kingship was undone by disobedience. At Gilgal he presumed to offer the sacrifice rather than wait for Samuel, and was told his kingdom would not continue (1 Samuel 13:13-14). Then, commanded to devote Amalek to destruction, he spared King Agag and the best of the spoil. Samuel’s verdict was final: “To obey is better than sacrifice… Because you have rejected the word of the LORD, he has also rejected you from being king” (1 Samuel 15:22-23).

God sent Samuel to anoint David in Saul’s place, “for the LORD sees not as man sees… the LORD looks on the heart” (1 Samuel 16:7). The Spirit departed from Saul and a harmful spirit tormented him, soothed only by David’s harp (1 Samuel 16:14). Consumed by jealousy after the women sang David’s praises, Saul hurled his spear at him and hunted him for years (1 Samuel 18:7-11; 1 Samuel 19:10). His descent grew darker — the slaughter of the priests of Nob (1 Samuel 22:18-19) and, on the eve of his last battle, a forbidden visit to the medium at Endor (1 Samuel 28:7).

Saul fell with his sons at Mount Gilboa, taking his own life rather than be captured by the Philistines (1 Samuel 31:4-6). The chronicler sums up his end: he died “for his breach of faith… because he did not keep the command of the LORD” (1 Chronicles 10:13). Saul stands as Scripture’s tragic warning that outward stature and office cannot replace a heart that obeys God — the foil to David, the man after God’s own heart, through whose line the Messiah would come.
